anki.deckName: Corebook::Neuro::Brain::Primary Effects of CNS Trauma::Acute Subdural Hematoma

imageRegistry.caption: Graphic shows an acute subdural hematoma (aSDH) compressing the left hemisphere & lateral ventricle, resulting in midline shift. Coexisting cortical contusions & axonal injuries are common in aSDHs. Contrecoup contusions are present.

visualEvidence.duplicateEvidenceRole: Use this plain/unannotated preview for visual duplicate classification before archiving any image.

imageRegistry.caption: NECT in a 58-year-old woman with head trauma shows a classic aSDH extending over left convexity & compressing the underlying subarachnoid space . Subtle hypodense foci within hyperdense aSDH represent unclotted blood & risk for rapid hematoma expansion.

visualEvidence.duplicateEvidenceRole: Use this plain/unannotated preview for visual duplicate classification before archiving any image.

imageRegistry.caption: NECT shows the small SDH is easier to see with wider windows (R) compared to standard brain window (L). Note cortical swelling under the SDH with disproportionate mass effect on lateral ventricle and midline shift of the septum pellucidum , concerning for potentially catastrophic brain swelling.

visualEvidence.duplicateEvidenceRole: Use this plain/unannotated preview for visual duplicate classification before archiving any image.

imageRegistry.caption: Axial T1 MR shows a crescentic left convexity aSDH (24 hours) . Blood is isointense to white matter but well seen due to ease of visualization of the brain surface. Note the subfalcine herniation .

visualEvidence.duplicateEvidenceRole: Use this plain/unannotated preview for visual duplicate classification before archiving any image.

imageRegistry.caption: Coronal graphic depicts a thin aSDH layering along the falx and tentorium . Such aSDHs may be difficult to identify on axial images alone. Multiplanar reconstructions are very helpful in identifying small, thin aSDHs.

visualEvidence.duplicateEvidenceRole: Use this plain/unannotated preview for visual duplicate classification before archiving any image.

imageRegistry.caption: Coronal NECT in a 41-year-old trauma patient shows an acute left falcine and tentorial SDH . Falcine and tentorial SDHs do not usually require surgery. There is also a right subgaleal scalp hematoma . Fractures may be present but are more commonly associated with acute epidural hematomas.

visualEvidence.duplicateEvidenceRole: Use this plain/unannotated preview for visual duplicate classification before archiving any image.

imageRegistry.caption: Axial NECT shows a 78-year-old patient with aSDH after a fall, which appears heterogeneous and contains several isodense foci , suggesting rapid hemorrhage with accumulation of unclotted blood. Note significant mass effect and midline shift related to brain edema, suggesting a potentially poor prognosis without aggressive treatment.

visualEvidence.duplicateEvidenceRole: Use this plain/unannotated preview for visual duplicate classification before archiving any image.

imageRegistry.caption: Follow-up CTA in the same patient shows globular enhancement within the aSDH, related to a spot sign representing active extravasation of hemorrhage.

visualEvidence.duplicateEvidenceRole: Use this plain/unannotated preview for visual duplicate classification before archiving any image.

imageRegistry.caption: NECT 24 hours later in same patient shows an increase in size of the aSDH , which is now more homogeneously hyperdense. Notice the increased cerebral mass effect, sulcal effacement, and rightward midline shift of the septum pellucidum and subfalcine herniation . Cranial decompression was subsequently performed.

visualEvidence.duplicateEvidenceRole: Use this plain/unannotated preview for visual duplicate classification before archiving any image.

imageRegistry.caption: aSDHs can be isodense to underlying brain, as in this anemic patient with aSDH with inward displacement of gray-white interface and rightward midline shift .

visualEvidence.duplicateEvidenceRole: Use this plain/unannotated preview for visual duplicate classification before archiving any image.

imageRegistry.caption: Axial NECT shows a crescentic, homogeneously hyperdense extraaxial collection with compression and displacement of the underlying brain, findings typical of an aSDH. SDHs may cross suture lines but do not cross dural attachments. They are often more extensive than epidural hematomas.

visualEvidence.duplicateEvidenceRole: Use this plain/unannotated preview for visual duplicate classification before archiving any image.

imageRegistry.caption: Axial NECT in a patient with minor head trauma could easily be called normal. There is a thin hyperdensity along the left tentorium compared to the right .

visualEvidence.duplicateEvidenceRole: Use this plain/unannotated preview for visual duplicate classification before archiving any image.

imageRegistry.caption: Reformatted coronal NECT in the same patient shows scalp contusion with a high-density foreign body . Note the slight increase in thickness and density of the left tentorium compared to the right .

visualEvidence.duplicateEvidenceRole: Use this plain/unannotated preview for visual duplicate classification before archiving any image.

imageRegistry.caption: Sagittal reformatted NECT through the left tentorial leaf shows a 2-mm thin aSDH .

visualEvidence.duplicateEvidenceRole: Use this plain/unannotated preview for visual duplicate classification before archiving any image.

imageRegistry.caption: Sagittal reformatted NECT through the right side of the tentorium shows the normal thin, slightly hyperdense dura . Thin, isolated peritentorial aSDHs do not expand and typically do not require follow-up.

visualEvidence.duplicateEvidenceRole: Use this plain/unannotated preview for visual duplicate classification before archiving any image.

imageRegistry.caption: Axial NECT in an anticoagulated obtunded 74-year-old woman with head trauma shows a large left-sided aSDH with such severe subfalcine and transtentorial herniation that the temporal horn of the left lateral ventricle is displaced completely into the tentorial incisura. The temporal horn of the right lateral ventricle is enlarged because the foramen of Monro is obstructed by the herniating brain.

visualEvidence.duplicateEvidenceRole: Use this plain/unannotated preview for visual duplicate classification before archiving any image.

imageRegistry.caption: Axial NECT in the same patient through the lateral ventricles shows the lateral ventricles are completely herniated under the falx, and the foramen of Monro is secondarily obstructed . The massive aSDH is heterogeneous and contains several isodense foci , suggesting rapid hemorrhage with accumulation of unclotted blood.

visualEvidence.duplicateEvidenceRole: Use this plain/unannotated preview for visual duplicate classification before archiving any image.

imageRegistry.caption: Coronal NECT shows the total herniation of the lateral ventricles under the falx. The temporal horn of the left lateral ventricle is nearly in the midline . The patient died shortly after the scan was obtained.

visualEvidence.duplicateEvidenceRole: Use this plain/unannotated preview for visual duplicate classification before archiving any image.

imageRegistry.caption: Axial T1 MR shows an acute right SDH. The collection is isointense with cortex. Contrast with left-sided hypointense chronic SDH is seen.

visualEvidence.duplicateEvidenceRole: Use this plain/unannotated preview for visual duplicate classification before archiving any image.

imageRegistry.caption: Axial T2 MR in the same patient shows the aSDH is profoundly hypointense while the chronic left SDH is hyperintense relative to brain.

visualEvidence.duplicateEvidenceRole: Use this plain/unannotated preview for visual duplicate classification before archiving any image.

imageRegistry.caption: Axial NECT scan in a 31-year-old man with minor trauma shows subtle hyperdensity along the tentorium and inferior falx near the confluence of the venous sinuses.

visualEvidence.duplicateEvidenceRole: Use this plain/unannotated preview for visual duplicate classification before archiving any image.

imageRegistry.caption: Coronal reformatted NECT from the axial data in the same patient shows the small acute peritentorial SDH . A small subtle convexity aSDH is seen here and was not visible on the axial scan.

visualEvidence.duplicateEvidenceRole: Use this plain/unannotated preview for visual duplicate classification before archiving any image.

imageRegistry.caption: Sagittal NECT scans in the same patient show the right peritentorial aSDH (top) with normal thin, hyperdense left sagittal dura (bottom) for comparison.

visualEvidence.duplicateEvidenceRole: Use this plain/unannotated preview for visual duplicate classification before archiving any image.

imageRegistry.caption: Axial NECT scan in a 30-year-old man with head trauma shows a small, mixed-density aSDH over the left frontal and parietal lobes. Subtle hypodensity within the aSDH was concerning for rapid accumulation of unclotted blood.

visualEvidence.duplicateEvidenceRole: Use this plain/unannotated preview for visual duplicate classification before archiving any image.

imageRegistry.caption: The same patient deteriorated 5 hours later. The aSDH has expanded significantly, and there are multiple foci of hypodensity within the hematoma indicating rapid bleeding. Note the subfalcine herniation of the lateral ventricles .

visualEvidence.duplicateEvidenceRole: Use this plain/unannotated preview for visual duplicate classification before archiving any image.

imageEvidence.purpose: Visual evidence for duplicate detection. Use these image files before deciding that source images are exact duplicates.
imageEvidence.duplicateClassificationRule: Archive only images proven to be exact/same-slice/same-screenshot duplicates or unusable. Caption-only similarity is not enough; uncertain or merely similar images stay primary as recognition reinforcement.
